subsections.label: Intuition First
subsections.heading: What is the Derivative?
subsections.body: Calculus is the science of change, developed to deal with constantly changing values like the position and velocity of moving objects. The derivative is fundamentally the exact rate of change — the slope of a function at one specific, instantaneous point.

analogy.title: The Driving Analogy (Distance vs. Velocity)
analogy.body:
Imagine you are driving. If your distance traveled over time is represented by a function f(t), subtraction gives you the change in distance between two times. The average velocity is simply the change in distance divided by the change in time.

But what if you want to know what your speedometer read at exactly 2:00 PM? Average velocity over an hour will not tell you.

The derivative is the mathematical tool that shrinks that time window down to zero, giving you the instantaneous speed on your dashboard.
keyPoints: The Microscope Equation: zoom in on any smooth curve far enough and it looks like a straight line — that line is the tangent, and its slope is the derivative.
The secant line gives average rate of change. The tangent line gives instantaneous rate of change.
f'(x) = lim(h to 0) [f(x+h) - f(x)] / h — this is the core definition.

definitions.definition: A line passing through two points on a curve. Its slope [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h is the average rate of change over the interval h — an estimate, not the exact slope.
[f(x+h) - f(x)] / h — measures the slope of the secant line between x and x+h. Taking its limit as h→0 yields the derivative.
f'(x) = lim(h→0) [f(x+h) - f(x)] / h. As h shrinks to zero, the secant line becomes the tangent line, giving the exact instantaneous slope at x.
The line that touches a curve at exactly one point (locally) with the same slope as the curve at that point. Its slope is the derivative f'(x₀).
f'(x) — read 'f prime of x'. The most common notation for the derivative.
dy/dx or df/dx. Here dx is an infinitely tiny change in x, and dy is the corresponding change in y. Emphasises the ratio-of-infinitesimals interpretation.
f(b) ≈ f'(a)(b - a) + f(a). Any smooth curve looks like a straight line when zoomed in sufficiently, so the tangent line at a known point a estimates f at nearby b.
The property that any smooth curve, zoomed in far enough at a point, appears to be a straight line. This is the geometric reason the derivative is a useful local approximation.
A point where the derivative does not exist — at sharp corners (|x| at x=0), vertical tangents (x^(1/3) at x=0), or discontinuities. A function can be continuous but not differentiable.

examples.title: Derivative of f(x) = x² from the Limit Definition
examples.problem: Find f'(x) for f(x) = x² using the difference quotient.
steps: Setup the difference quotient: [f(x+h) - f(x)] / h
Expand the binomial: [x² + 2xh + h² - x²] / h
Cancel the x² terms: [2xh + h²] / h
Factor out h from the numerator: h(2x + h) / h = 2x + h
Take the limit as h → 0: lim(h→0) [2x + h] = 2x

examples.title: Finding the Slope at a Specific Point
examples.problem: Find the instantaneous slope of f(x) = x² - 2x + 1 at x = 1.6.
steps: Differentiate each term: d/dx[x²] = 2x, d/dx[-2x] = -2, d/dx[1] = 0.

examples.title: Equation of the Tangent Line
examples.problem: Find the tangent line to y = x⁴ - x² + 3 at x = 1.
steps: Find the slope at x = 1: f'(1) = 4(1)³ - 2(1) = 2. Slope m = 2.

subsections.label: Visual
subsections.heading: Visual Understanding
visualNotes: Secant to Tangent: As the second point slides toward the first, the secant line pivots and becomes the tangent line at the limit.
Graphing the Derivative: When f(x) is at a peak or valley (slope = 0), f'(x) = 0. When f is rising steeply, f'(x) is large and positive.
Non-Differentiable Corners: f(x) = |x| has a sharp V at x=0. No single tangent line fits on that tip — the derivative does not exist there.
Use the slider to move the tangent point along the curve and watch the slope value change in real time.

snippets.title: Derivative from Limits
snippets.code:
from sympy import symbols, limit

x, h = symbols('x h')

# Define f(x) = x^2
f_x   = x**2
f_x_h = (x + h)**2

# Difference quotient
diff_quotient = (f_x_h - f_x) / h
print("Difference quotient:", diff_quotient)

# Limit as h -> 0
derivative = limit(diff_quotient, h, 0)
print("Derivative of x^2:", derivative)

snippets.title: Fast Differentiation
snippets.code:
from sympy import symbols, diff

a = symbols('a')
f = a**2

# Differentiate
df = diff(f, a)
print("f'(a) =", df)          # 2*a

# Slope at a specific point
slope_at_minus3 = df.subs(a, -3)
print("Slope at a=-3:", slope_at_minus3)  # -6

# More complex function
g  = a**4 - a**2 + 3
dg = diff(g, a)
print("g'(a) =", dg)
print("g'(1) =", dg.subs(a, 1))

snippets.title: Tangent Line
snippets.code:
from sympy import symbols, diff

a  = symbols('a')
f  = a**2
df = diff(f, a)

# At x = 2
x0 = 2
m  = df.subs(a, x0)   # slope = 4
y0 = f.subs(a, x0)    # y = 4
b  = y0 - m * x0      # y-intercept = 4 - 8 = -4

print(f"Tangent point : ({x0}, {y0})")
print(f"Slope m       : {m}")
print(f"Tangent line  : y = {m}*x + ({b})")

questions.text: What is the formal limit definition of the derivative f'(x)?
questions.explanation: The derivative is the limit of the difference quotient as h→0 (not x→0). The h is the tiny gap we shrink; x is the fixed point where we measure the slope.

questions.text: If f(x) = x², what is f'(3)?
questions.explanation: f'(x) = 2x for f(x) = x². Substituting x = 3 gives f'(3) = 2(3) = 6. The slope of the parabola at x = 3 is 6.

questions.text: A function is continuous at x = 0. Can you conclude it is differentiable there?
options: No — continuity is necessary but not sufficient for differentiability
Yes — if it has no jumps it must have a defined slope
No — differentiability implies continuity, not the other way around
questions.explanation: Differentiability implies continuity, but NOT vice versa. f(x) = |x| is continuous everywhere yet fails to be differentiable at x = 0 because of the sharp corner.

questions.text: The Microscope Equation says f(b) ≈ f'(a)(b-a) + f(a). What is this doing geometrically?
options: Computing the exact value of f at b using integration
Approximating f(b) using the tangent line at the known point a
Finding the secant slope between a and b
Computing the second derivative at a
questions.explanation: The Microscope Equation is local linearisation — it uses the tangent line at a (slope f'(a), through (a, f(a))) to estimate f at a nearby point b. Exact only at a; accuracy decreases as b moves farther away.

questions.text: What does the Mean Value Theorem guarantee for a function continuous and differentiable on [a, b]?
options: The function reaches its maximum somewhere in (a, b)
There is at least one point c in (a, b) where f'(c) equals the average rate of change over [a, b]
The function is constant on [a, b]
The derivative is zero somewhere in (a, b)
questions.explanation: The MVT guarantees a point c where instantaneous slope equals average slope [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a). Classic analogy: if your average speed was 75 mph, at some instant your speedometer read exactly 75 mph.

problems.label: Concept Check
problems.problem: What is the fundamental difference between the expression [f(x+Δx) - f(x)] / Δx and the actual derivative f'(x)?
problems.hint: One is a ratio over a finite interval; the other involves a limit.
problems.solution: The difference quotient is an average rate of change over a finite interval Δx — it is an estimate (the secant slope). The derivative f'(x) is the exact instantaneous rate of change obtained by taking the limit as Δx → 0. The derivative is the limit; the quotient is the approximation.

problems.label: Application
problems.problem: Using the limit definition of the derivative, find f'(x) for f(x) = 3x².
problems.hint: Write [3(x+h)² - 3x²]/h, expand, cancel, then take h→0.
problems.solution:
1. Setup: lim(h→0) [3(x+h)² - 3x²] / h
2. Expand: [3x² + 6xh + 3h² - 3x²] / h
3. Cancel 3x²: [6xh + 3h²] / h
4. Factor h: 6x + 3h
5. Limit h→0: f'(x) = 6x

problems.label: Thinking-Based
problems.problem: Prove algebraically using left-hand and right-hand limits why f'(0) does not exist for f(x) = |x|, even though the function is continuous at x = 0.
problems.hint: Write |0+h| for h>0 and h<0 separately. The function is piecewise: x for x≥0, -x for x<0.
problems.solution:
Right-hand limit (h > 0): lim [|0+h|/h] = lim h/h = 1.
Left-hand limit (h < 0): lim [|0+h|/h] = lim (-h)/h = -1.
Since 1 ≠ -1, the limit does not exist. f'(0) is undefined despite continuity — the sharp corner prevents a unique tangent line.

problems.label: Physics
problems.problem: The distance of a moving car in miles over time t (hours) is f(t) = t². Using the Microscope Equation, estimate the distance traveled exactly 1 minute (1/60 hours) after t = 1 hour.
problems.hint: Use f(b) ≈ f'(a)(b-a) + f(a). Let a=1 and b=1+1/60.
problems.solution:
f'(t) = 2t. At a=1: f(1)=1 mile, f'(1)=2 mph.
Microscope Equation: f(1+1/60) ≈ 2·(1/60) + 1 = 1/30 + 1 ≈ 1.0333 miles.
The car travels approximately 0.0333 miles (about 176 feet) in that minute.

mistakes.title: Setting h = 0 Too Early
mistakes.description: The most critical error: substituting h = 0 into the difference quotient before doing algebra. This gives 0/0 immediately. You must first expand the numerator, cancel the h algebraically, and only then take h → 0.

mistakes.title: Continuity Does Not Guarantee Differentiability
mistakes.description: Every differentiable function is continuous, but not every continuous function is differentiable. f(x) = |x| is perfectly continuous everywhere but not differentiable at x = 0 (sharp corner). f(x) = x^(1/3) has a vertical tangent at x = 0 — also non-differentiable.

mistakes.title: Confusing Average and Instantaneous Rate
mistakes.description: The difference quotient [f(x+h)-f(x)]/h is an AVERAGE rate over the interval h. Only after taking the limit as h→0 do you get the INSTANTANEOUS rate. Using the quotient directly (without the limit) to describe 'the slope at a point' is incorrect.

insights.title: The Mean Value Theorem (MVT)
insights.body: If a function is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), there exists at least one c in (a,b) where f'(c) = [f(b) - f(a)] / (b - a). The instantaneous slope at c equals the average slope over the whole interval. If your average highway speed was 75 mph, the MVT guarantees your speedometer read exactly 75 mph at least once.

insights.title: L'Hopital's Rule
insights.body: When evaluating limits of ratios f(x)/g(x) that produce the indeterminate form 0/0 (or inf/inf), take the derivative of numerator and denominator separately: lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(x)/g'(x). When functions race toward zero, their slopes (derivatives) control the final ratio. This is a direct application of derivative thinking.

applications.description: If f(t) is an object's position over time, then f'(t) is its velocity and f''(t) is its acceleration. Derivatives are the native language of motion — from falling objects to orbital mechanics.

In economics, 'marginal' literally means derivative. Marginal cost is the derivative of the total cost function — the cost of producing one more unit. Setting the profit function's derivative to zero finds the optimal production level.

Training a neural network minimises a loss function. The direction to update each weight is given by the gradient — a vector of partial derivatives. Gradient descent iteratively steps in the direction of steepest descent using derivatives at every step.

Engineers use the Microscope Equation constantly to approximate complex nonlinear systems over tiny intervals as simple straight lines — turning intractable equations into solvable ones. Control systems, signal processing, and structural analysis all rely on this technique.
